name: Xochipilli
description: Xochipilli, whose name translates to "Prince of Flowers" or "Noble Flower," is a prominent deity in the Aztec pantheon, associated with art, games, dance, flowers, pleasure, and feasting. He embodies the exuberance of youth and the intoxicating beauty of the natural world. Xochipilli's influence extended to the realm of hallucinogenic plants, suggesting a connection between ecstatic experiences and artistic inspiration. He is often depicted adorned with flowers, butterflies, and mushrooms, visually representing his association with the transformative power of nature and altered states of consciousness. As a patron of artists and craftspeople, Xochipilli inspired creativity and skill. His worship involved elaborate rituals, including feasts, dances, and the consumption of psychoactive substances, aimed at achieving communion with the divine and fostering artistic expression. Xochipilli's presence underscored the importance of joy, beauty, and artistic creation within Aztec society, highlighting the interconnectedness of nature, art, and spirituality.
subtitle: Prince of Flowers

keyMyths.title: Xochipilli's Hallucinogenic Trance
keyMyths.description: According to some interpretations, Xochipilli's pose, particularly his open mouth and upturned face, suggests a state of ecstatic trance induced by the consumption of hallucinogenic plants. This trance facilitated communication with the divine and inspired artistic vision. His image, adorned with psychoactive flora, reinforces this connection, linking artistic inspiration to altered states of consciousness and the power of the natural world.
keyMyths.source: Wasson, R. Gordon. Soma: Divine Mushroom of Immortality. Harcourt Brace Jovanovich, 1968.

keyMyths.title: Xochipilli and Xochiquetzal
keyMyths.description: Xochipilli is often associated with Xochiquetzal, the goddess of beauty, love, and female sexuality. While not always considered consorts, their shared domain over pleasure and beauty suggests a complementary relationship. They represent the dual aspects of artistic inspiration: Xochipilli embodying the masculine, active principle, and Xochiquetzal representing the feminine, receptive principle.
keyMyths.source: Miller, Mary, and Karl Taube. An Illustrated Dictionary of the Gods and Symbols of Ancient Mexico and the Maya. Thames & Hudson, 1993.

keyMyths.title: The Feast of Xochipilli
keyMyths.description: The festival dedicated to Xochipilli involved elaborate rituals, including dances, music, and the offering of flowers and incense. Participants would often consume pulque (a fermented beverage) and possibly other psychoactive substances to enhance their connection to the deity. The feast was a time of celebration, artistic expression, and communal bonding, fostering a sense of unity and shared joy.
keyMyths.source: Sahagún, Bernardino de. General History of the Things of New Spain. Translated by Arthur J.O. Anderson and Charles E. Dibble. University of Utah Press, 1950-1982.

sources.author: Wasson, R. Gordon
sources.description: Explores the role of psychoactive substances in religious and cultural practices, offering insights into the possible significance of hallucinogenic plants in Xochipilli's iconography.
